crits.summary: The spec nails the technical hard parts (no double-booking, VPR, Xendit, PWA/Lighthouse) but is dangerously thin on the operational policies that actually make or break a court business: cancellation/no-show/refund rules, slot/duration model, maintenance blocks, and the cold-start of a two-sided marketplace. As written, a venue owner could not safely run day-to-day operations or trust the payout, and the 1%-only revenue model plus full-prepay requirement will make supply-side acquisition very hard in Jakarta 2026. It is build-ready as a booking engine but not as a marketplace business; several P0 policy/economic gaps must be specified before code.

findings.area: Cancellation / No-show / Refund policy (booking lifecycle)
findings.issue: The spec lists status `cancelled`/`no_show` and says 'refund path on cancellation per policy' and 'refund' as a payments line item, but the policy itself is entirely undefined. There is no cancellation window (e.g. free >24h, 50% 6-24h, 0% <6h), no rule on who eats the Xendit fee on refund, no no-show definition/enforcement, no rule for venue-initiated cancellations (rain-out, court damage, power cut — extremely common in Jakarta), and no late-arrival/grace policy. This is THE single most operationally load-bearing policy for a court business and the entire reason operators currently demand manual transfer + screenshot (so they control the money). With full prepay and no defined refund rules, the first rain-out or court closure becomes a manual support fire and a chargeback/dispute magnet. VPR also references 'no_show' nowhere, and Open Match has no abandonment policy when a 4th player drops.
findings.recommendation: Add a first-class CancellationPolicy entity (per-venue, with platform defaults): tiered refund % by time-to-start, explicit Xendit-fee bearer, venue-initiated-cancellation = 100% auto-refund + optional credit, configurable grace period, and no-show auto-marking (e.g. 15 min past start, staff-overridable). Define Open Match drop/under-fill behavior (auto-cancel + full refund if not filled by T-Xh; partial-fill substitution). Make refund mechanics (Xendit refund vs platform credit vs next-booking discount) explicit, since Indonesian QRIS/e-wallet refunds are slow/partial and operators will prefer credit. This must be specified before any booking code.

findings.area: Slot/duration model, half-court, and minimum-booking economics
findings.issue: `pricing_rules.slot_minutes (e.g. 60/90)` and `bookings.duration_min` imply fixed slot lengths, but real Jakarta padel/tennis operations book in 60/90/120-min blocks AND need: per-court different default durations, buffer/turnaround time between bookings, minimum booking duration enforcement, and crucially the padel norm of selling per-court (4 players split cost off-platform) vs the platform's Open Match per-head model. Tennis is often booked 60-min, padel often 90-min — the slot grid generation logic, peak/off-peak band boundaries crossing a single booking (a 90-min booking spanning peak→off-peak), and how price is computed when a booking straddles two pricing bands are all unspecified. 'Half-court' / shared-court is mentioned in the lens but absent in the model. Without this, the availability grid and price are ambiguous and venues can't model their real inventory.
findings.recommendation: Specify slot generation explicitly: per-court bookable duration options, configurable inter-booking buffer (cleaning/turnaround), min/max duration, and a deterministic rule for price when a booking crosses pricing bands (e.g. price = sum of per-15-min segment rates, or 'band of start time wins' — pick one and test it). Confirm v1 sells whole courts only (recommended) and that Open Match is the only per-head path; defer half-court. Add `buffer_min` to courts and make the availability engine band-aware. This is a hard blocker for the booking module.

findings.area: Marketplace cold-start / two-sided trust (go-to-market is unaddressed)
findings.issue: The spec is a pure feature list with zero strategy for the chicken-and-egg problem, which is the #1 reason court marketplaces fail. In Jakarta 2026, venues already have full WhatsApp/IG booking funnels and no incentive to hand their schedule (and 1% + payout timing risk) to a new platform; players won't come without supply. The spec even market-frames the problem correctly ('utilisation/off-peak, no-shows, discovery') but then builds a generic booking engine that does NOT preferentially solve off-peak filling (no off-peak-specific demand generation, no last-minute deal surfacing — explicitly deferred to Phase 3 dynamic pricing). VPR/Open Match is positioned as the differentiator/wedge but is buried as module 8 with no launch sequencing. There's also no migration path for a venue's EXISTING bookings/customers, which operators will absolutely require before switching.
findings.recommendation: Add a go-to-market/cold-start section: launch wedge = VPR + Open Match as the demand-generation hook (players come for ratings/matches, not generic booking), seed 5-10 anchor venues hand-onboarded with concierge support, and explicitly make off-peak Open Match the utilisation play in v1 (it already fills empty off-peak courts — lead with it). Require an import path for a venue's existing forward bookings + a 'powered by your IG/WA' deflection so the venue keeps its brand. Without a stated wedge and supply-acquisition plan, the build is a solution looking for a marketplace.

findings.area: Court maintenance blocks, holds, and schedule overrides
findings.issue: There is no entity or flow for blocking court time outside of a booking: maintenance, resurfacing, private events/tournaments, owner personal use, weather closures, public holidays, and temporary hour changes. operating_hours is a static weekday table with no date-specific override (Idul Fitri, Christmas, Independence Day all change hours), and no concept of a one-off closure. In practice operators block courts constantly; if the platform can take a paid booking on a court the owner has closed for resurfacing, that's a guaranteed refund + reputation hit on opening week. This is a routine, daily operational must-have that's entirely missing.
findings.recommendation: Add a `court_blocks` (or `availability_exceptions`) entity: court_id/venue_id, starts_at/ends_at, reason (maintenance|event|holiday|weather|private), recurring?, that the availability engine treats as unavailable. Add date-specific `operating_hours` overrides (holiday calendar) layered over the weekday defaults. Surface block creation in the venue dashboard calendar. Ensure the Redis-locked booking flow checks blocks, not just bookings, before creating a hold.

findings.area: Payout timing, settlement risk, and the 1%-only revenue model
findings.issue: Two coupled business risks. (1) Economics: 1% of GMV is extremely thin for a marketplace that must fund Xendit MDR (QRIS ~0.7%, e-wallet/VA fees), WhatsApp message costs, support, and CAC — at 1% the platform likely loses money per transaction after payment processing, since Xendit's own fee often exceeds 1%. The spec says 'venue payout = transaction − 1%' but never states who absorbs the Xendit MDR; if it's the platform, gross margin is negative. (2) Payout/settlement: 'Nightly job ... Xendit disbursement (or manual for v1)' plus venue 'receives payout' with no stated settlement frequency, no T+N schedule, no held-funds/float model, no handling of refunds that occur AFTER a payout was already remitted (clawback), and no failed-disbursement retry. Operators will not move volume onto a platform whose payout timing and reconciliation are vague — they currently get cash instantly via transfer. PPN invoice is mentioned but the actual tax treatment (is VenuePro a payment facilitator? marketplace VAT collector? whose NPWP/e-faktur?) is hand-waved.
findings.recommendation: Separate platform commission from payment processing: state explicitly whether Xendit MDR is passed to the venue, the player, or absorbed — and recompute unit economics (likely need 3-5% commission OR a flat per-booking fee, not 1%, for v1 viability; pressure-test with the 1% deduction Xendit fee math). Define a concrete payout schedule (e.g. T+2 weekly or daily settlement), a held-funds window covering the cancellation period to enable clawback before remittance, refund-after-payout handling, and failed-disbursement retries. Get a real tax position (PPN as marketplace operator, e-faktur responsibility, whether commission is the taxable base) reviewed before launch — this affects invoice and payout schema.

findings.area: VPR cold-start vs Jakarta reality (verified-match bottleneck and adoption)
findings.issue: VPR only counts verified matches (booked + played at venue + score confirmed), and the primary feed is Open Match. At launch there will be near-zero Open Matches, so VPR has nothing to calibrate from for weeks/months, making the 'UTR-grade rating' differentiator hollow at exactly the moment it's needed as the wedge. Doubles rating from team-average is also notoriously noisy with sparse data — 10 provisional matches is optimistic for a doubles-only padel ladder. Meanwhile, both-sides-confirm friction (4 players must confirm a score) will see low completion in casual social padel where people leave immediately after the game. There's no plan for importing existing ratings (players who already have rough self-levels) beyond self-assessment, and no anti-collusion for friends boosting each other in low-density early networks.
findings.recommendation: Add a bootstrap plan: allow venue-staff single-side verification (already half-mentioned) as the DEFAULT for v1 to cut confirmation friction; seed initial R from a structured self-assessment + an optional in-person calibration session at anchor venues; widen provisional window and RD for doubles. Sequence Open Match heavily in marketing so match volume exists. Add collusion guards beyond gap-damping (e.g. cap rating gain frequency between the same player pairs in a low-N network). Be explicit that VPR is 'calibrating/low-confidence' in public UI for the first N weeks to manage trust.

findings.area: Venue onboarding / opening-day operations and dashboard sufficiency
findings.issue: Onboarding is one admin bullet ('onboard/moderate venues') with no defined operator setup flow: KYC/bank-account verification for payouts (mandatory before any disbursement), how a venue's many courts + complex peak/off-peak/weekend/holiday pricing get entered without hours of data entry, staff account provisioning, and a go-live checklist. The venue dashboard module lists calendar/bookings/walk-in/pricing/payouts but omits daily operational must-haves: a day-view 'who's coming today' sheet for front desk, manual booking edit/move/extend, partial-payment or pay-at-venue option (many Jakarta venues still want cash/deposit-only), booking search by phone, and an offline/poor-signal mode for front desk (the PWA-offline is aimed at players, not the staff terminal which most needs it). Walk-in entry exists but there's no reconciliation of cash collected vs platform records.
findings.recommendation: Specify a venue onboarding flow with bank/KYC verification gating payouts, a fast pricing setup (templates: copy-court, bulk peak/off-peak, weekend multiplier, holiday calendar), and staff invites. Expand the dashboard spec for front-desk reality: today day-sheet, move/extend/edit booking, search-by-phone, mark cash-paid walk-ins with a cash reconciliation line in the payout report, and offline resilience for the staff calendar. Add a concierge/manual-onboarding path for anchor venues in v1.

findings.area: Pricing/availability under contention and overbooking edge cases
findings.issue: Redis lock + DB transaction handles two users racing for the same slot, but several real-world contention cases are unspecified: a walk-in created by staff at the front desk while an online hold exists on the same slot (staff path bypasses the online flow), a hold that expires precisely as payment succeeds (race between expiry job and webhook), Open Match per-head holds (4 separate payments — is the COURT held while waiting for all 4 to pay, blocking normal bookings, and what's the TTL?), and clock/timezone correctness for WIB slot boundaries around DST-free but holiday-shifted hours. The 10-min hold is also long for hot peak slots in a scarce-supply market and will frustrate buyers; conversely Open Match needs a much longer fill window. There's no overbooking/oversell guard if Xendit webhook is delayed past hold expiry but payment actually went through (player paid, slot released, double-sold).
findings.recommendation: Specify staff-vs-online contention (staff booking must also acquire the same lock and respect active holds, with an explicit override + audit), the expiry/webhook race resolution (webhook wins; if slot was re-sold, auto-refund the late payer with priority), Open Match court-hold semantics and TTL (long fill window with a single court hold, not 4), and idempotent late-payment reconciliation. Make hold TTL configurable per context (short for single bookings, long for Open Match). Add a 'paid-but-released' safety net that refunds and notifies rather than silently double-selling.

findings.area: Trust, safety, and dispute handling for players and money
findings.issue: For a two-sided marketplace handling real money and in-person meetups, several trust elements are absent from v1: no dispute/complaint flow for players (booking not honored, court double-sold, refund not received) beyond the implicit VPR score dispute; no venue verification badge / quality moderation depth beyond pending/active/suspended; no player no-show consequence to protect Open Match groups (one no-show ruins a 2v2 for three others); no review system (explicitly deferred — acceptable, but means zero quality signal at launch, which hurts discovery trust); and no fraud controls on OTP-only accounts (one phone = one identity, throwaway numbers for Open Match abuse/refund fraud). For Jakarta specifically, WhatsApp-OTP-only with no email creates account-recovery risk when users change numbers.
findings.recommendation: Add a minimal v1 trust layer: a booking-dispute channel (even if WhatsApp-to-support + admin tool), Open Match no-show penalty (e.g. temporary Open Match suspension or forfeited fee to the group), basic OTP abuse rate-limiting + device/number reputation, and an account-recovery path (secondary email or admin-assisted). Keep venue reviews deferred but add a lightweight verified-venue badge and a clear refund-status indicator players can self-serve. These protect the marketplace's reputation during the fragile launch window.

crits.lens: Senior Laravel 12 architect (concurrency, multi-tenancy, payments, PostGIS, queues, Livewire, testability)
crits.summary: The product/data model is coherent and the major risks are named (double-booking, webhook idempotency, tenant scoping), but the actual integrity mechanisms are under-specified to the point of being unsafe to build as written. The single biggest gap is reliance on a Redis advisory lock plus an application-level re-check to prevent double-booking with no database-level overlap constraint — a guaranteed double-booking under lock loss, app crash, or expiry races. Several other areas (hold-expiry vs payment-time coupling, tenant-scope leakage via relations/admin bypass, slot-vs-range modeling, payout reconciliation) need concrete constraints and state machines before implementation.

findings.area: Double-booking prevention (DB integrity)
findings.issue: The only line of defense is a Redis lock `lock:court:{id}:{slotStart}` plus an in-transaction re-check. This is NOT a correctness guarantee. Concrete failure modes: (1) Redis lock TTL expires mid-payment (hold is 10min but a single-key SETNX TTL is 'short') while the holder still believes it owns the slot; a second player acquires the same key and both create `hold` rows. (2) Redis failover/restart drops the lock (Redis locks are non-durable; Redlock is explicitly not safe for correctness per its own author). (3) The re-check `SELECT ... WHERE status IN (hold,paid,confirmed)` is a read that does not block a concurrent inserter in READ COMMITTED — two transactions can both see 'no conflict' and both INSERT. (4) Walk-in path and online path race because walk-in skips the Redis lock entirely in 6.6. There is no UNIQUE or EXCLUDE constraint, so the DB will happily store two overlapping bookings.
findings.recommendation: Make the database the source of truth. Add `tstzrange` (or generated range from starts_at + duration) and a PostGIS-independent exclusion constraint: `CREATE EXTENSION btree_gist; ALTER TABLE bookings ADD CONSTRAINT no_overlap EXCLUDE USING gist (court_id WITH =, tstzrange(starts_at, starts_at + (duration_min || ' minutes')::interval) WITH &&) WHERE (status IN ('hold','paid','confirmed'));`. This makes overlap a hard `23P01` error regardless of Redis. Keep Redis only as a fast-fail UX optimization, not the guarantee. Catch the exclusion-violation exception and return 'slot just taken'. Ensure walk-in inserts go through the same constraint. Confirm slot_minutes/duration alignment so a 90-min booking correctly blocks overlapping 60-min slots (range overlap handles this; discrete-slot UNIQUE would not).

findings.area: Hold expiry vs payment window coupling / orphaned holds
findings.issue: `hold_expires_at = now+10min` but Xendit QRIS/VA/e-wallet payment windows are frequently longer (VA can be hours; QRIS dynamic ~ up to expiry you set). The spec has no rule tying the Xendit charge expiry to the 10-min hold. Failure: hold expires and the slot is released (job), then the player pays the still-open Xendit charge → webhook arrives `paid` for a `cancelled`/released booking whose slot is now taken by someone else → money captured for an unavailable court. Also, the 'release slot (job)' is time-based and racy against an in-flight webhook (webhook lands at T+10:00.1 after the release job at T+10:00.0).
findings.recommendation: Set the Xendit charge expiry equal to (or slightly less than) the hold window and pass it explicitly. On webhook `paid`, do an atomic conditional transition `UPDATE bookings SET status='confirmed' WHERE id=? AND status='hold' AND hold_expires_at > now()` inside a transaction; if 0 rows affected, treat as late payment → auto-refund via Xendit and notify. Make the expiry job also conditional (`WHERE status='hold' AND hold_expires_at <= now()`) and idempotent. Define the late-payment refund policy explicitly (who eats the Xendit fee). Document the exact ordering and the compensating refund path; this is a money-loss bug if left to 'release job + cancel pending payment'.

findings.area: Webhook idempotency & ordering
findings.issue: Spec says 'idempotent, signature-verified' and has a `webhook_events` table, but gives no concrete idempotency key, no handling of out-of-order/duplicate delivery, and no guard against processing a `paid` then a later `expired`/`failed` for the same charge. Xendit retries webhooks and can deliver duplicates and out-of-order. Without a unique constraint on the provider event id and a state-machine guard, retries can double-enqueue WhatsApp + double-trigger payout aggregation, and a late `failed`/`expired` could cancel an already-confirmed booking.
findings.recommendation: Add `webhook_events (provider, event_id UNIQUE, payload, processed_at)` and insert-first with a unique constraint; on duplicate `event_id`, no-op. Verify Xendit `x-callback-token`/signature before processing. Process inside a DB transaction keyed by booking with a status guard so terminal states (`confirmed`,`cancelled`,`refunded`) are not overwritten by stale events (define an explicit allowed-transition matrix). Make all downstream effects (WA send, payout inclusion) idempotent on `payment_id`. Add a feature test that replays the same webhook 3x and an out-of-order paid-then-failed sequence.

findings.area: Row-level multi-tenancy via global scope — leakage & admin bypass
findings.issue: `venue_id` global scope + 'platform admin bypasses the scope' is a classic leak surface. Concrete pitfalls: (1) The public marketplace MUST query across all venues, so the scope cannot be unconditionally on `Venue`/`Court`/`Booking` — meaning the team will toggle it on/off by context, and any forgotten toggle leaks or over-restricts. (2) Global scopes do NOT propagate through relationships loaded from an already-fetched parent, and are bypassed by `DB::table`, `whereHas` subqueries, raw PostGIS queries, and `firstOrCreate` race inserts — all of which appear in discovery/search here. (3) 'Admin bypass' usually means `withoutGlobalScopes()`, which is all-or-nothing and easy to leave on. (4) Models without a direct `venue_id` (bookings have court_id; player_ratings/matches/open_matches are cross-venue) won't be covered by a `venue_id` scope at all.
findings.recommendation: Decide tenancy boundary explicitly per model: public read models (venues/courts/availability) are NOT tenant-scoped — they are policy-gated for writes only. Tenant-scope ONLY the dashboard write/read surface, ideally by binding the current venue from the route, not an implicit global scope. For bookings, scope via `court.venue_id` (a relation), so add a denormalized `venue_id` on `bookings` to make the constraint and scope cheap and indexable. Replace 'admin bypass' with explicit policies (`Gate::before` for super-admin) rather than `withoutGlobalScopes`. Add tests that assert a venue-staff token cannot read/write another venue's bookings, payments, payouts, and that raw PostGIS discovery queries do not accidentally apply (or skip) the scope. Note open_matches/matches/player_ratings are platform-global by design — document that they are intentionally un-scoped.

findings.area: PostGIS + full-text search 'availability' filtering
findings.issue: Discovery filters by 'availability' (6.3 lists search by availability) but availability is a computed function of operating_hours minus bookings minus holds over a requested time window — it is not a column. Doing geo-radius + full-text + real-time availability in one indexable SQL query is hard: you cannot put availability in a GiST/GIN index. Naively this becomes a per-venue N+1 availability computation after the geo filter, which kills the Lighthouse/TTFB target on the most important public page. Also unspecified: SRID/geography vs geometry (radius in meters needs `geography` or ST_DWithin with a cast), and which FTS (Postgres `tsvector` vs separate engine).
findings.recommendation: Specify: use `geography(Point,4326)` + `ST_DWithin(geom, point, radius_m)` for correct metric radius, GiST index on geom. Use a `tsvector` generated column + GIN for name/amenity search. Treat availability as a two-stage filter: geo+text+price in SQL to get candidate venues, then a bounded availability check against a precomputed/cached per-court slot bitmap in Redis (the 'hot availability' cache already mentioned) rather than live SQL per venue. Define cache invalidation on booking create/cancel/expire. Add the slot-generation algorithm (how operating_hours + pricing_rules.slot_minutes produce concrete slots, including DST-free WIB but day-boundary and 90-min-over-close handling).

findings.area: Open Match multi-party payment atomicity & refunds
findings.issue: 4 players each pay per-head independently, and only at 4-paid does the match become `full`→`confirmed` and teams get balanced. Unspecified failure modes: player 4 never pays (3 are out-of-pocket and the slot is held) → refund all 3; a player pays after the slot's hold expired; two matches competing for the same court slot; partial-fill timeout; what blocks the underlying court booking while the match is filling. The booking that the open match occupies needs the same overlap constraint, and the per-head payments need a saga/refund-on-timeout, none of which is designed.
findings.recommendation: Model the open match as holding a single `booking` (status=hold) on the court for the whole window via the same exclusion constraint, with a fill deadline ≤ hold expiry. Track per-participant payment state; on deadline-not-full, auto-refund all paid participants (Xendit refund) and release the booking — define who pays Xendit fees. Add an explicit state machine open→filling→full→confirmed→completed/cancelled with idempotent transitions and concurrency guard on the join (so a 5th payer can't slip in). Decide team-balancing tie-breaks deterministically for testability.

findings.area: VPR rating engine — concurrency, idempotency & spec ambiguities
findings.issue: Glicko-2 is inherently rating-period/batch based; applying it 'per match' (as 6.3 states) is a known deviation that affects RD/sigma math and the cited fairness invariants. Concurrency: two confirmed matches for the same player processed by parallel queue workers will read-modify-write `player_ratings` and lose updates (no row lock or version mentioned). 'Idempotent per match' needs a concrete guard (the `rating_history` unique-on-(match_id,user_id) is the natural lock but isn't stated as a constraint). The formula mixes a logistic expectation with Glicko-2's own g(RD)/E — the spec conflates Elo-with-margin and Glicko-2 without stating the canonical update.
findings.recommendation: Pin the algorithm precisely (it is effectively Glicko-2 with a margin-of-victory weight; write the exact step). Define the rating-period strategy (per-match online update is acceptable but document the σ/RD update you actually use). Enforce idempotency with a UNIQUE(match_id,user_id) on rating_history and wrap updates in `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` on the 4 player_ratings rows (ordered by user_id to avoid deadlock) or serialize rating jobs per-user via a single-worker queue/lock. Keep the engine a pure function (state in, deltas out) so the golden-case unit tests in §10 are real; the persistence/locking is the integration layer.

findings.area: Payout reconciliation, PPN correctness & money safety
findings.issue: '1% fee' and 'gross − 1% = net' is under-specified for real money: is the 1% on gross including or excluding the Xendit MDR (QRIS ~0.7%, VA flat, e-wallet %)? If platform fee is only 1% but Xendit takes ~0.7-2%, the platform can net negative on some methods. PPN (11-12%) handling is hand-waved: is displayed price PPN-inclusive, who is the taxable entity (platform vs venue), and is the 1% fee itself subject to PPN? Refunds/cancellations after a payout period are not reconciled (clawback). Nightly aggregation over `paid` bookings can include rows that later refund.
findings.recommendation: Define the fee waterfall explicitly: gross → Xendit MDR (record per-payment actual fee from webhook, do not estimate) → platform 1% (state base) → venue net. Store the real provider fee on `payments`. Decide PPN treatment with finance (most likely: venue is the merchant of record for the court service PPN; platform issues a PPN invoice on its 1% service fee) and put PPN fields on both `payments` and `payouts`. Add cancellation/refund clawback so a refund after payout adjusts the next period. Aggregate payouts off an immutable ledger (append-only `ledger_entries`) rather than re-summing mutable bookings, for auditable reconciliation.

findings.area: Slot/booking data model, timezones & Livewire dashboard testability
findings.issue: Several modeling gaps: bookings store `starts_at` + `duration_min` but no `ends_at`/range column (needed for the exclusion constraint and overlap queries). No explicit timezone discipline — store UTC, render WIB; operating_hours are local wall-clock and must be reconciled to bookings' timestamptz. The booking status enum lacks `payment_pending`/`expired` distinctly (failed vs expired vs user-cancelled collapse). On Livewire: the venue calendar is a high-write, multi-user surface; without `wire:poll` discipline + optimistic-lock/version on bookings, two staff editing the calendar will silently clobber. No mention of how Livewire components stay performant against the Lighthouse ≥98 budget on the dashboard (Livewire ships its runtime + per-action round-trips).
findings.recommendation: Add a generated `tstzrange` column (or `ends_at` + computed range) and index it. Mandate `timestamptz` everywhere, app timezone UTC, display WIB; convert operating_hours to concrete UTC instants during slot generation. Expand the booking state machine and document allowed transitions. For the calendar, add an integer `version`/`updated_at` optimistic-lock check on writes and surface conflicts. Note dashboard Lighthouse is less critical (authed, non-indexed) but still budget Livewire payload; keep Alpine for local interactions. Add explicit DB indexes list (bookings(court_id, range), payments(provider_ref), webhook_events(event_id) unique, venues geom GiST, ratings(user_id,sport,discipline) unique).

crits.summary: v1 is well-architected and internally coherent, but it is over-scoped for a single build: it bundles five independent hard systems (locked booking+online payment, marketplace payout+PPN tax, a UTR-grade rating engine, Open Match matchmaking, and a venue dashboard) behind brutal non-functional gates, while quietly betting the whole thing on two-sided liquidity it has no plan to bootstrap. The riskiest assumption is unstated: that Jakarta venues will let a brand-new platform take 100% of payment up front and own the payout/cashflow, and that enough players will adopt to make VPR and Open Match work — neither has a cold-start plan. The truly thinnest launchable slice is single-venue/concierge discover→book→pay→WhatsApp with manual payout; VPR and Open Match should move to a fast-follow phase gated on real booking liquidity.

findings.area: Scope sizing / what is actually in v1
findings.issue: v1 claims to be the 'core transaction loop' but actually bundles FIVE independent, individually-hard subsystems: (1) Redis-locked booking + full online payment with hold/expiry/refund, (2) marketplace payout with 1% fee + PPN e-invoicing + Xendit disbursement, (3) a UTR-grade Glicko-2 rating engine with margin/doubles/anti-gaming, (4) Open Match matchmaking (create/join/pay-per-head/team-balance/score-confirm), and (5) a Livewire venue dashboard — ALL behind hard non-functional gates (Lighthouse >=98 all categories, installable PWA + offline, fully self-hosted assets). Sprint 1-2 is named as the container but no single team ships payments+tax+rating+matchmaking+dashboard+PWA-perf in two sprints. Each of (2),(3),(4) is a multi-week project on its own. This is 3-4 distinct epics labelled as one.
findings.recommendation: Re-baseline v1 around ONE provable thesis: 'kill the WhatsApp+manual-transfer booking flow.' Cut v1 to modules 1-6 + 9 + 11 (identity, venue/court, discovery, booking, payments+MANUAL payout, notifications, dashboard, PWA shell). Move VPR (module 7) and Open Match (module 8) to v1.5, and full automated payout+PPN disbursement to v1.5. Rename the current document to 'product vision' and write a separate, smaller 'v1 build spec' so the team is not committing to a vision-sized scope under a sprint-sized label.

findings.area: Two-sided liquidity / cold-start (riskiest assumption)
findings.issue: The single most important risk for any marketplace — how you bootstrap two-sided liquidity — is completely absent from the spec. v1 assumes venues will list AND players will discover/book, but there is no go-to-market, no seeding strategy, no 'who is venue #1.' Worse, several v1 features are liquidity-gated and will look broken at launch: Open Match needs ~4 same-level players free at the same slot/venue (impossible with <hundreds of active players per area); VPR needs a volume of confirmed matches to produce non-noise ratings; discovery/radius search and AggregateOffer look empty with 1-3 venues. Building matchmaking before you have players is building the third floor before the foundation.
findings.recommendation: Make the launch strategy explicit and supply-led: hand-onboard 3-8 anchor venues in one Jakarta cluster (e.g. one neighborhood) before any player acquisition, and treat the first venues as concierge/done-for-you. Define a written liquidity threshold (e.g. 'do not enable Open Match in an area until N>=X active rated players and >=Y venues') and gate the matchmaking UI behind it so it is hidden, not empty. Add an explicit cold-start section to the spec; without it the rating + open-match investment is dead weight at launch.

findings.area: Sequencing / dependency chain
findings.issue: The feature dependency graph is deep and partly circular, but v1 schedules everything in parallel: Open Match depends on booking+payment (per-head charge) AND on VPR (level matching ±0.5, team balancing); VPR depends on Open Match for its primary calibration data ('Calibrated from Open Match results'); both depend on the booking/lock loop being rock-solid. So VPR and Open Match are mutually bootstrapping (rating needs matches, matchmaking-by-level needs ratings) and BOTH sit downstream of the riskiest piece (money movement). Shipping them concurrently means debugging matchmaking, payment, and a noisy rating engine simultaneously with no stable substrate, and the seed-rating quality problem (everyone starts at self-assessed R with high RD) makes early ±0.5 matching effectively random.
findings.recommendation: Sequence in three gates: (G1) booking+payment+notifications+dashboard, proven with real money and no double-booking; (G2) once real bookings flow, add 'friendly match with confirmed score' as the FIRST rating source (no matchmaking needed — players just book normally and record a score), letting VPR calibrate on organic data; (G3) only after a rating base exists, ship Open Match using real ratings for level-matching. This breaks the circular dependency (matches before matchmaking) and means the rating engine is tuned on real distributions before anyone relies on it for pairing.

findings.area: Payments / payout — venue trust & cashflow assumption
findings.issue: The economic core rests on an unvalidated behavioral assumption: that Jakarta venues will let a brand-new, unproven platform collect 100% of the booking amount up front and control the payout cycle (nightly aggregation -> period payout -> disbursement). Today venues get paid instantly via direct bank transfer/screenshot. Switching to 'a startup holds your money and pays you on a cycle' is a hard sell and a regulated activity (holding/transmitting customer funds, escrow-like flows, Xendit KYB/aggregator terms, PPN withholding). The spec treats this as a feature checkbox, not as the make-or-break commercial + compliance risk it is. 'Manual payout for v1' is mentioned in passing but the operational, legal, and reconciliation implications are not specified.
findings.recommendation: De-risk the money model before building automation: (a) confirm Xendit's marketplace/sub-account/aggregator product and whether VenuePro is merchant-of-record or facilitator — this changes who is liable for PPN and refunds; (b) for v1, explicitly choose split-on-settlement or fast (e.g. T+1/per-booking) payout to reduce the 'you're holding my money' objection, and document manual payout as the real v1 path with a defined SLA, not a fallback; (c) validate willingness-to-accept the 1% + delayed-payout model with the anchor venues before committing engineering. Consider whether v1 could even launch as 'platform shows availability, payment optionally direct-to-venue via Xendit sub-account' to lower the trust barrier.

findings.area: VPR rating engine scope vs. value at launch
findings.issue: VPR is the most engineering-intensive, correctness-critical module (Glicko-2 + margin + doubles team-average + anti-farming + decay + provisional + idempotent history + exhaustive golden tests) yet delivers ~zero user value until there is match volume — and at launch there will be almost none. Doubles rating from team-average is a known-hard, under-researched problem (UTR/DUPR have spent years on it); building a novel 'UTR-grade' engine as part of a v1 sprint, then having it produce visibly noisy/unfair numbers on thin early data, risks burning credibility on the platform's stated differentiator. The spec also under-specifies key parameters: K, alpha, w weights, RD floors/ceilings, decay rate, the 'sets below threshold' rule, and how singles tennis even occurs in a doubles-oriented Open Match product.
findings.recommendation: Pull VPR out of v1. Ship a non-rated v1 and add VPR in v1.5 once organic confirmed-score matches exist to tune against. When you do build it, start with the SIMPLEST defensible model (e.g. transparent Elo-with-margin) rather than Glicko-2+volatility, validate fairness invariants on real data, and only add doubles team-handling/anti-gaming once singles works. Specify all tunables and a calibration plan (offline backtest on collected scores) before coding. This also removes VPR from the critical path of the money-making loop.

findings.area: Open Match scope / payment + refund edge cases
findings.issue: Open Match multiplies the hardest part of the system — money — by four and adds combinatorial failure modes the spec hand-waves. 'Each joiner pays per-head; when 4 paid -> full -> confirmed' implies four separate Xendit charges against one slot, but the spec doesn't address: what happens when 3 pay and the 4th never does (refund the 3? hold the slot? for how long?), partial-fill expiry, a player paying then dropping after 'full', no-shows in a paid 4-person match, and disputes over score-confirmation when one of four refuses to confirm. Each of these is a refund/payout/rating-integrity problem layered on top of the base booking loop, and they will generate the bulk of support load — for a feature that, per the liquidity finding, mostly can't fill at launch anyway.
findings.recommendation: Defer Open Match to v1.5 (after liquidity + a working rating base). When specified, fully enumerate the partial-fill/refund/no-show/dispute state machine BEFORE building, decide the slot-hold economics (who funds the empty-slot risk while waiting for the 4th), and consider a much simpler v1.5 'pickup' primitive (organizer pays for the whole slot, collects from friends off-platform, marks a score) before building per-head escrow matchmaking. Do not ship per-head payment + team-balancing + 4-way confirmation in the same release as the base payment loop.

findings.area: Non-functional gates as scope risk
findings.issue: The hard gates — Lighthouse >=98 across ALL categories on mobile, installable PWA with offline shell, fully self-hosted libraries/fonts/assets (zero CDN), AA a11y, Lighthouse CI fail-under-98 — are each real work and interact badly with the dynamic, auth'd, Livewire-heavy dashboard and the Xendit/WhatsApp/map third-party widgets. A >=98 'all categories' gate that fails the build is a chronic schedule risk (a single regressed metric blocks merges), and 'no external CDN' collides with embedded Google Maps discovery and any hosted payment/WA UI. These constraints are correct for the PUBLIC marketing/discovery pages but are being applied as if uniformly across an app that includes a logged-in SPA-like dashboard.
findings.recommendation: Split the gate by surface: apply Lighthouse >=98 + SSR + offline only to public discovery/venue/court pages (the SEO + acquisition surface that actually needs it); apply a looser, realistic budget to the authenticated dashboard. Resolve the 'no CDN' vs Google Maps tension explicitly (self-host map tiles via an alternative, or accept the maps origin as a documented exception). Make Lighthouse CI a warning on first violation and a hard block only on the public-pages job, so a perf regression in the dashboard doesn't freeze unrelated feature work.

findings.area: Walk-in / offline-booking parity (under-specified, high real-world value)
findings.issue: The spec's own market analysis says the operator's real pain is utilisation, no-shows, and that booking today is WhatsApp + manual transfer. The feature that directly addresses 'stop double-booking and replace the WhatsApp ledger' is the venue-side calendar + walk-in/phone entry — arguably the highest-certainty value in the whole product and the strongest reason a venue adopts at all. Yet walk-in is treated as a one-line sub-step of the booking flow, while two liquidity-gated, lower-certainty features (VPR, Open Match) get full sections. There's also no handling for the venue's existing bookings/migration, overlapping manual+online writes to the same slot, or the no-show problem the market section explicitly calls out.
findings.recommendation: Elevate the venue calendar + walk-in/phone booking to a first-class v1 pillar and spec it properly (manual block-out, recurring-looking manual entries, conflict handling between staff entry and live online bookings, no-show marking that feeds nothing-yet-but-later-VPR). Make the single venue value prop 'one source of truth for your court schedule + online deposits to cut no-shows' rather than 'rating + matchmaking.' This is the feature most likely to win the first 5 venues and should be the spine of the thin slice.

crits.lens: Rating systems expert (Glicko-2 / UTR / Elo) — VPR fairness & correctness
crits.summary: Section 7 advertises Glicko-2 but specifies an Elo/Glicko-1 update (R' = R + K·g(RD)·w·(S−E)); these are mathematically different systems and the spec never defines the actual Glicko-2 machinery (μ/φ transform, variance v, Δ, the iterative σ' Illinois update, rating-period batching), so "VPR is Glicko-2" is currently unimplementable as written. Nearly every parameter that determines fairness — α, K, w, the g(RD) form, σ/τ, RD floor/ceiling, decay rate, and the games-share → win-probability mapping — is left as a symbol or "≈", and the team-average-then-distribute doubles model has a known statistical bias (it credits all teammates equally regardless of individual RD and lets one rating absorb a partner's error). It is build-ready as a vision but not as a formula; below are concrete formulas and defaults to pin it down before any code is written.

findings.area: Engine identity: Glicko-2 claimed but Elo formula given
findings.issue: Section 7 says 'Glicko-2 + margin + doubles' and lists R, RD, σ, but the per-player update is `R' = R + K·g(RD)·w·(S_A − E_A)` — that is Elo (Glicko-1 at best). True Glicko-2 has no K, no direct g(RD)·(S−E) term. It (a) converts R→μ=(R−1500)/173.7178 and RD→φ=RD/173.7178, (b) computes estimated variance v from opponents, (c) computes the improvement Δ = v·Σ g(φ_j)(s_j−E_j), (d) updates volatility σ' via the iterative Illinois/regula-falsi root-find on f(x) using system constant τ, (e) updates φ* = sqrt(φ²+σ'²), φ' = 1/sqrt(1/φ*² + 1/v), and μ' = μ + φ'²·Σ g(φ_j)(s_j−E_j). None of this is in the spec. As written the engine is underdetermined: you cannot implement 'Glicko-2' from this section, and the 'σ (volatility)' column in player_ratings is never actually used by the given formula. Also Glicko-2's whole design is rating-PERIOD based (batch N matches, then one update); the spec implies a per-match online update (6.3 'On match=confirmed'), which is a different, less stable estimator and changes how RD/σ evolve.
findings.recommendation: Decide explicitly between two coherent designs and write the full equations: OPTION A (recommended, simplest correct): drop the Glicko-2 label, ship a margin-aware Glicko-1-with-volatility-omitted or an Elo+RD hybrid, and remove σ from the model. OPTION B (keep Glicko-2): specify all five steps above and adopt the standard internal scale (operate in μ/φ at base 400/173.7178 internally, map to 0–7 only for DISPLAY). Pin defaults: τ (system constant) = 0.5 (lower = less volatile; use 0.3–0.5 for a recreational pool), initial σ = 0.06, internal rating range mapped so 0–7 display spans μ∈[−Z,+Z] (see mapping finding). If per-match updates are required for UX immediacy, define a 'rating period' = 1 match but document that this inflates RD/σ noise, and compensate by raising τ damping or batching nightly. State clearly which one v1 builds; do not ship a spec that names one system and specifies another.

findings.area: Expected-score curve: α≈1.0 on a 0–7 scale is far too steep
findings.issue: `E_A = 1/(1 + 10^(−(Rteam_A − Rteam_B)/α))` with α≈1.0 means a 1.00-VPR gap → E=0.909, a 1.5 gap → E=0.969, a 2.0 gap → E=0.990. So the system treats a half-level gap as nearly a coin flip only within ~0.3 VPR, and anything past 1.0 VPR as an almost-certain blowout. This is internally inconsistent with the product: Open Match matches within ±0.5 VPR (6.2), and the 'mismatch damping at |gap|>2.0' rule is nearly dead because E is already ~0.99 there — there is essentially zero expected information left to update on, so wins yield ~0 by construction (which they then double-damp). It also makes the scale violently sensitive: one upset across a 1.0 gap produces a large (S−E) and, multiplied by K and g(RD), a big swing. Empirically, real racket-sport rating gaps of ~1 level correspond to roughly 70–80% game-win expectancy, NOT 91%.
findings.recommendation: Calibrate α to the 0–7 scale so that the curve matches observed game-win rates. Target: a 1.0-VPR gap ≈ 0.76 game-win expectancy (matches padel/tennis empirics) and a 2.0-VPR gap ≈ 0.92. That requires α ≈ 2.0 for base-10 logistic: E=1/(1+10^(−Δ/2)) gives Δ=1→0.76, Δ=2→0.91, Δ=0.5→0.64. Equivalently in natural-log/Elo terms use a scale c where 1 VPR ≈ 200 Elo points. Pin α = 2.0 as the default and make it a tunable constant calibrated against backtested Open Match data after ~3 months. Add an explicit invariant test: 'players within ±0.5 VPR have E∈[0.5,0.64]' to lock the matchmaking band to a meaningful competitiveness.

findings.area: Doubles model: team-average-then-distribute is statistically biased
findings.issue: The spec uses Team R = mean(players) and then applies the SAME scalar update to each teammate (6.3 'per-player Glicko-2 step'), with only RD scaling differing. Two known problems. (1) IDENTIFIABILITY / credit assignment: averaging discards the information that a strong+weak pair beat an even pair primarily because of the strong player; the mean treats them as one blended entity, so the weak player is over-credited and the strong under-credited symmetrically every match — this is the classic 'carry' exploit the spec claims to prevent but the averaging math actually enables. The spec's mitigation ('being carried yields small gains because team was favoured') only fires when the pair is rated as favoured; against equal-rated opponents a 2.0+5.0 pair vs two 3.5s is rated even, so the 2.0 gets a FULL share of any win. (2) VARIANCE: an individual's update should be modulated by how much HIS rating (vs his partner's) was responsible, which requires a partner-strength-relative term, not a flat split. The 'g(RD) scaling' partially helps (high-RD player moves more) but does not fix the structural bias because it scales magnitude, not direction/attribution.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a teammate-aware attribution, not a flat split. Concrete options, in order of preference: (1) BEST — model each side's effective rating as a configurable combine that is NOT a plain mean, e.g. weighted toward the stronger player to reflect padel/tennis reality (the better player touches more balls): Rteam = 0.6·R_high + 0.4·R_low for doubles (tune w_combine). Then distribute the update inversely to confidence: ΔR_i ∝ g(RD_i)/Σg(RD_j) (more-uncertain player absorbs more), AND apply a partner-gap correction so the lower-rated player's gain is capped when the team was favoured by his partner: gain_i ← gain_i · clamp(1 − β·max(0, R_partner − R_i)/scale, floor, 1). (2) Treat doubles rating as a SEPARATE latent (already in the data model: discipline=doubles) and never let doubles results touch singles — good, the spec does this. Pin defaults: w_combine = 0.6/0.4, β = 0.25, floor = 0.3. Add golden tests: a 2.0 player repeatedly partnered with a 6.0 beating 4.0/4.0 pairs must converge BELOW 4.0, not toward 4.0.

findings.area: Margin mapping: raw games-share S = games_A/(games_A+games_B) is wrong as a win-probability target
findings.issue: S_A = games_A/(games_total) is used directly as the 'actual score' against a logistic E that models WIN probability. These are different quantities. A 6–4, 6–4 win is 12/20 = 0.60 games-share, but it is a 100%-confident WIN of the match — feeding 0.60 as 'actual' against E (often 0.5–0.7) can produce a NEGATIVE update for the team that actually WON if they were favored, which is perverse for a recreational audience ('I won and my rating dropped'). UTR deliberately uses a percent-of-games model but pairs it with a different expectation curve and reliability weighting; you cannot mix UTR's actual with Elo's expected without calibrating both to the same units. Also unaddressed: tennis sets/tiebreaks (a 7–6 tiebreak set is 7 games but a near-coin-flip), padel golden point, and super-tiebreak third sets — all distort raw games-share. No floor/cap on S (a 6–0,6–0 = 1.0 share creates maximal swings against provisional players).
findings.recommendation: Separate two signals and combine: (a) match outcome W∈{1,0} (or 0.5), (b) margin m = games-share, mapped through a compression so blowouts don't dominate. Use S = W·(0.5 + κ·(margin−0.5)) clamped to [0.05,0.95] with κ≈0.5 so a win is always ≥0.5 (you can never lose rating for winning) and a 6–0 6–0 maps to ~0.85–0.90 not 1.0. Equivalently adopt UTR-style: actual = games_won/games_played but then ALSO gate the update by match result so winners never get negative deltas, and weight by 'competitiveness' (closer matches carry more info per the spec's own philosophy — actually invert: a blowout is LOW information about the favorite, HIGH about the underdog). Define tennis tiebreak handling explicitly (count a tiebreak set as 1 game margin, or use games within the set, not 7–6). Define a games threshold below which a match is 'incomplete' and excluded (e.g. < 6 total games / retirement before set 1 complete) — the spec's 'Sets below threshold excluded' names no threshold.

findings.area: Every key parameter is a symbol or '≈' — K, g(RD), w, RD bounds, decay all undefined
findings.issue: Section 7 leaves the entire parameter set unpinned: α'≈1.0' (wrong, see above), K never given, g(RD) described only as 'larger when uncertain' (no functional form), w only as 'set format/competitive/recency' (no values), RD has no floor/ceiling, σ/τ absent, inactivity decay direction stated but no rate, provisional 'first 10 matches' has no defined faster-update mechanism (just 'fast swings'). This is not implementable and, worse, not TESTABLE — Section 10 promises 'exhaustive unit tests with golden cases / provisional convergence / decay' but you cannot write a golden case without the constants. A reviewer cannot judge fairness of unspecified numbers.
findings.recommendation:
Pin a concrete default table (display scale 0–7, internal Elo-scale 0–1400 where 1 VPR=200 pts):
• α (logistic, base-10, VPR units) = 2.0.
• If Glicko-1/Elo hybrid: K_base = 0.15 VPR (=30 Elo) for established; g(RD) = 1/sqrt(1 + 3·q²·RD²/π²) with q=ln(10)/400 (standard Glicko g), RD in internal units.
• If true Glicko-2: no K — use the μ/φ update above; τ=0.4.
• RD floor = 0.30 VPR (=60 Elo) — never let a rating become unfalsifiable; RD ceiling = 1.75 VPR (=350 Elo) = a fresh player.
• Initial RD = 1.75 VPR (max uncertainty); self-assessment seeds R only, RD stays max.
• σ initial = 0.06, bounded [0.03, 0.10].
• Provisional: n_matches < 10 → multiply effective K (or skip RD floor) so RD decays fast; flag 'calibrating'. After 10, normal floor applies.
• w (match weight): friendly/Open Match = 1.0, tournament = 1.25, league = 1.15. Recency handled via RD growth, not w.
• Inactivity decay: RD grows per Glicko-2 between rating periods via σ, OR explicit RD ← min(RD_ceiling, sqrt(RD² + (c²·days_idle))) with c chosen so RD goes from floor to ~1.0 VPR after ~6 months idle (c² ≈ (0.7²)/180 per day). Do NOT decay R itself (UTR doesn't); only widen RD.
Make all of these named config constants with a single source of truth, and require a golden-test per constant.

findings.area: Scale mapping 0–7 and cross-discipline/cross-sport comparability undefined
findings.issue: The 0.00–7.00 display is asserted but never tied to the internal engine: what internal rating = VPR 3.5? Is the mapping linear, clamped, or compressed at the tails? Without a fixed anchor, two problems: (1) ratings can drift off the 0–7 range (the update formula has no boundary, so a dominant player exceeds 7.0 or a beginner goes negative — needs explicit clamp + how clamping interacts with the update, since naive clamping breaks zero-sum and lets a 7.0 player farm points that vanish). (2) Padel-doubles VPR, tennis-singles VPR, and tennis-doubles VPR are three INCOMPARABLE latent scales but share one 0–7 label and one matchmaking UI; a 4.0 padel player and 4.0 tennis-singles player are not equivalent, and Open Match (padel) vs any tennis cross-listing will mis-seed. Self-assessment seeding across three scales with one slider is ambiguous.
findings.recommendation: Fix an explicit anchored mapping: define VPR = clamp(2.5 + (μ_internal − μ_ref)/scale, 0, 7) where μ_ref is the population median anchored to VPR 2.5–3.0 (recreational median) and scale set so 1 VPR = 200 internal pts. Handle the boundary by clamping DISPLAY only while keeping internal μ unbounded (so a 7.0+ player still gains/loses internally and zero-sum is preserved). Maintain THREE independent internal scales (padel-doubles, tennis-singles, tennis-doubles), each with its own population anchor (do not assume identical medians). Seed each separately from self-assessment with max RD. Label the UI per discipline ('Padel VPR 4.0') and never cross-match disciplines. Add a periodic re-anchoring job (recompute μ_ref = population median monthly) so the 0–7 band stays meaningful as the population grows — and document that re-anchoring shifts everyone's displayed number, which needs a comms plan.

findings.area: Gaming vectors: collusion, soft-throwing, partner-stacking, and 2-of-4 confirmation are under-defended
findings.issue: Anti-gaming relies on 'verified (booked+played+confirmed)' + diminishing returns + anti-sandbag, but several concrete attacks remain open. (1) COLLUSION RING: 4 friends repeatedly play real, paid, confirmed Open Matches and feed predetermined scores to pump one member's VPR before a tournament/ladder — every check passes (real booking, both sides 'confirm' because all are in on it). Margin-aware actually HELPS the attacker (they can dial precise scores). (2) SOFT-THROW / sandbag: provisional 10-match window with 'fast swings' is the easiest sandbag window — lose 10 on purpose to seed low, then win a bracket; the spec's 'losses to weaker still drop you' doesn't prevent deliberate early low seeding because there's no asymmetry between provisional gains and losses. (3) PARTNER-STACKING: as in the doubles finding, repeatedly partnering a weak account with a strong one to inflate the weak one. (4) CONFIRMATION CAPTURE: '(or venue staff verifies)' lets a single staff member rubber-stamp; 'both sides confirm' in a 2v2 is really 2-of-4 if one per side suffices — define quorum. (5) SELF-SEED INFLATION: self-assessment seeds R high → first matches against true-level opponents print losses but high-RD means small downside per the design; a vanity-seeder sits at an inflated number for many matches.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit defenses: (a) CONFIRMATION quorum = at least one player from EACH side AND not both from the same booking account; staff-verify allowed only for walk-in/dispute and logged/audited. (b) Cap rating GAIN per opponent-pair per rolling window: diminishing returns vs the SAME opponents (track recent head-to-head; the Nth match vs the same group within 30 days has w decayed, e.g. w·0.5^(repeats)). This directly defuses collusion rings — you can't pump by replaying the same 3 people. (c) Provisional asymmetry: during n<10, allow rating to MOVE freely but do NOT let a player ENTER a ranked ladder/tournament seed until RD < threshold (e.g. RD < 0.7 VPR) — sandbaggers can't cash in an uncalibrated low rating. (d) Self-assessment seeds R but UI shows 'unverified' until ≥5 confirmed matches; never use self-seed for matchmaking band tightness. (e) Anomaly flags for admin: score patterns with implausibly low variance, same-foursome frequency, large pre-event gain spikes. (f) Make per-match Δ history immutable + visible (already promised) — keep it as the audit trail. None of these need ML for v1; they're deterministic rules.

findings.area: Singles vs doubles cross-feed and tennis discipline separation gaps
findings.issue: Good: the data model has discipline=singles|doubles and the spec says tennis keeps them separate, padel doubles-only. But two gaps: (1) Section 6.3 and 7 describe a single 2v2 update path; the SINGLES (tennis) update path (1v1, no team averaging, straightforward Glicko) is never separately specified — yet it's in v1 scope ('tennis singles AND doubles'). The team-average formula degenerates oddly for n=1 (team=player, fine) but the doubles-specific damping/attribution logic must be bypassed for singles, which isn't stated. (2) A new tennis player has THREE cold starts to seed (none transfer); spec doesn't say whether playing tennis doubles informs tennis singles at all. UTR famously blends, but blending requires a cross-discipline covariance model that is explicitly out of scope here — so it must be stated as 'no transfer' to avoid implementer ambiguity.
findings.recommendation: Add a short 'Singles path' subsection: for discipline=singles, team size=1, skip combine/attribution, apply the plain (Glicko-1/2) update with the same α=2.0 and margin mapping. Explicitly state: NO rating transfer between sport/discipline scales in v1 (each of padel-doubles, tennis-singles, tennis-doubles is independent); a single self-assessment question per discipline at onboarding. Note as a Phase-5 candidate: optional weak prior transfer (e.g. seed tennis-doubles RD slightly tighter if tennis-singles is established) — but flag it out of v1 to keep the engine pure/testable as Section 10 demands.

crits.lens: Indonesia payments/fintech expert — money flow, licensing, settlement, tax, reconciliation
crits.summary: The money-flow section is the spec's biggest under-specified risk. As written ("player pays full → platform aggregates paid bookings nightly → gross −1% → disburse"), VenuePro is custodying and aggregating third-party funds, which is the exact pattern that draws PJP/escrow scrutiny under BI Reg 23/6/2021 (now PBI 10/2025) and triggers the PMK 37/2025 e-commerce 0.5% PPh-collector regime. The fix is to never touch the float: use Xendit xenPlatform split-rules (per-transaction split, venue funds land in the venue's Xendit-held sub-account at settlement, platform only ever receives its 1% fee) instead of pooling into the platform balance — and to separately resolve the QRIS T+2-vs-instant-confirmation float, refund-before-settlement, PPN-on-commission (PKP/e-faktur), and PMK-37 withholding obligations, none of which the spec currently addresses.

findings.area: Fund custody / licensing — does VenuePro hold third-party money?
findings.issue: Section 6.4 describes the platform collecting the full player payment, then a 'nightly job aggregates a venue's paid bookings ... gross − 1% = net → disbursement'. That is pooling/aggregating other people's money in the platform's own balance and paying it out later — i.e. acting as an escrow/fund-manager. Under BI Reg 23/6/PBI/2021 (and the late-2025 PBI 10/2025 + PADG 32/2025 framework), holding and managing source-of-funds on behalf of others is a licensed Payment Service Provider (PJP) activity; a non-licensed platform is 'not permitted to take deposits or directly manage funds' and must route funds through a licensed PJP. The spec never states which custody model Xendit is used in, so the default reading is the non-compliant one.
findings.recommendation: Make it an explicit architectural invariant that VenuePro NEVER holds or aggregates venue funds. Use Xendit xenPlatform with per-transaction split rules: at charge time the player's payment is split so the venue's net (99%) is credited to that venue's Xendit-held sub-account and VenuePro's 1% fee is credited to the platform account. Xendit (the licensed PJP) is the legal fund-holder; payout is just sub-account → venue bank disbursement. Drop the 'nightly aggregate gross then subtract fee' model entirely — it is both a licensing risk and an unnecessary float. Document the chosen xenPlatform account type (split-rule routing, not 'owned' pooling) in the spec and in Xendit onboarding. If business reasons require pooling, budget for the PJP licensing path (incl. 51% Indonesian voting control) before launch.

findings.area: Settlement timing vs instant booking confirmation (float + cashflow)
findings.issue: Flow 6.1 confirms the booking and fires WhatsApp the instant the success webhook arrives — correct UX. But QRIS settles T+2 business days on Xendit (e-wallet/VA similar T+1/T+2), and the spec treats 'paid' as if funds are available. Combined with 6.4's aggregate-then-disburse, this means VenuePro is promising a confirmed court immediately while the cash is still in transit, and any payout/refund that happens before settlement is being funded from float the platform may not legally hold. The spec has no concept of 'authorized/confirmed' vs 'settled', no settlement-status field, and no handling of the gap.
findings.recommendation: Split state cleanly: booking confirmation is driven by the payment-success webhook (instant); money movement is driven by a separate settlement event. Add settlement_status + settled_at to payments, and only allow disbursement/refund decisions that respect actual settled funds (xenPlatform split avoids this because the split happens at settlement into the venue sub-account — another reason to adopt it). Make payout cadence follow Xendit settlement (T+2+), not booking time. State the T+2 reality and weekend/holiday settlement explicitly so venues' payout expectations are set correctly.

findings.area: PMK 37/2025 — platform as 0.5% PPh income-tax collector
findings.issue: The spec's tax section only mentions 'PPN (11–12%) handling on invoices/payouts'. It completely omits PMK 37/2025 (Oct 2025), which appoints qualifying e-commerce/marketplace platforms (PPMSE) — explicitly those using an escrow/settlement mechanism and exceeding DGT transaction-value/traffic thresholds — as collectors of 0.5% PPh on each seller's gross turnover, with obligations to withhold, deposit to the State Treasury, file monthly returns, and issue withholding proof to sellers. VenuePro's full-amount-collection flow is precisely the escrow pattern the rule targets, and venues are the 'sellers'. This is a statutory collection/remittance obligation, not optional.
findings.recommendation: Add a tax-engine workstream: (1) determine whether VenuePro crosses the PMK 37/2025 appointment threshold (and treat appointment as a near-certain medium-term outcome); (2) capture each venue's NPWP and turnover-status; (3) implement the IDR 500m small-seller exemption (collect signed statements), the 0.5% withholding on gross-ex-VAT, monthly deposit/filing, and per-transaction bukti potong issuance; (4) reflect the withheld 0.5% in the payout breakdown (gross − 0.5% PPh − 1% platform fee = net) so payout math and venue invoices are correct. This materially changes the payout data model (payouts table needs pph_withheld, npwp, exemption_flag).

findings.area: PPN / e-faktur correctness on the 1% commission (not on the booking)
findings.issue: The data model puts invoice_no (PPN) on payouts and §6.4 issues a 'PPN e-invoice' at payout. This conflates two different taxable events. The court booking itself is the venue's sale to the player (venue's PPN obligation, if the venue is PKP). VenuePro's taxable supply is its 1% commission service to the venue — once VenuePro is PKP it must charge 11%/12% PPN on that 1% fee and issue a proper e-faktur to the venue (with the venue's NPWP), reportable in Coretax/e-faktur. The current design risks issuing a wrong/ambiguous invoice and mis-stating who owes PPN on what base.
findings.recommendation: Model two invoice streams: (a) the venue's booking receipt to the player (platform may generate it on the venue's behalf, clearly the venue's sale), and (b) VenuePro's commission tax-invoice to the venue for 1% + PPN on that 1%. Register VenuePro as PKP, integrate e-faktur/Coretax for the commission stream, store venue NPWP, and make the fee economics explicit: is the 1% inclusive or exclusive of PPN? (1% + 12% PPN = 1.12% effective haircut — decide and document, because it changes venue payout and the split-rule amount.)

findings.area: Refunds / cancellations — timing, fee reversal, and who funds them
findings.issue: The spec says only 'refund path on cancellation per policy' and lists a 'refunded' payment status, but there is no cancellation policy, no fee-on-refund rule, and no handling of refund-before-settlement. Open questions with money consequences: Is the 1% platform fee refunded too, or kept? QRIS/e-wallet refunds are often not free and can be slow or unsupported (some QRIS rails have no programmatic refund — only reversal or manual transfer). With the split model, refunding a venue's 99% after it has already settled into the venue sub-account requires clawing back from the venue, which the design doesn't address. No-show vs venue-cancel vs player-cancel have different money outcomes and none are specified.
findings.recommendation: Write an explicit cancellation/refund matrix: player-cancel (tiered % refund by lead time), venue-cancel (full refund + who eats the fee), no-show (forfeit), weather (padel/tennis outdoor). For each, specify: refundable amount, whether the 1% fee reverses, and the funding source. Prefer cancellation windows that keep refunds before settlement (cheaper, no clawback). Verify per-channel Xendit refund support (QRIS vs e-wallet vs VA differ) and capture refund fees. For post-settlement refunds in the split model, define the venue-balance clawback / negative-balance handling.

findings.area: Chargebacks, disputes, and failure/fraud handling
findings.issue: The spec assumes a clean success webhook and ignores adversarial money paths. QRIS/e-wallet/VA have low chargeback exposure vs cards, but Xendit still has dispute/reversal, partial-payment, overpayment (VA), late-payment-after-hold-expiry, and double-webhook scenarios. §6.1 expires the hold at 10 min and 'cancels pending payment', but a VA/QRIS payment can land AFTER the hold expired and the slot was released — now the player paid for a slot they don't have. There is no defined remedy (auto-refund vs auto-rebook vs credit), and no reconciliation of orphaned payments.
findings.recommendation: Define the 'paid-after-expiry' race explicitly: on late settlement of an expired hold, attempt re-acquire of the slot; if unavailable, auto-refund (or issue credit) and notify. Handle VA overpayment/underpayment, duplicate webhooks (idempotency keyed on Xendit event id — already partly noted), and Xendit dispute webhooks. Add a daily three-way reconciliation (bookings ↔ payments ↔ Xendit settlement report) with an exceptions queue for the platform admin; the spec mentions a 'reconciliation report' but defines no reconciliation source-of-truth or break-handling.

findings.area: Open Match split payments and refund fan-out (4 payers, 1 court)
findings.issue: Open Match collects per-head fees from up to 4 players for one slot that fills incrementally, then can fail to fill ('open'→ stays open) or be cancelled. This multiplies every money problem: 3 of 4 paid and the 4th never joins → 3 refunds (each with channel refund cost and T+2 settlement timing); team-balancing happens only at 'full', so funds are held across multiple players before the booking is even confirmed; the venue's 99% must still split correctly once confirmed. None of the refund-on-failed-fill, partial-fill expiry, or per-head fee reconciliation is specified, and the platform-fee base (1% of total court fee vs 1% per head) is undefined.
findings.recommendation: Specify Open Match money rules: a fill deadline after which an unfilled match auto-cancels and auto-refunds all paid participants; whether early payers' funds are held as authorizations vs captured charges (prefer not capturing until fill, if the channel supports it, to avoid refund churn); the exact fee base (1% of aggregate court fee); and the split-rule that routes 99% to the venue sub-account only on confirm. Add per-participant payment + refund status to open_match_participants and reconcile it to the single court booking.

findings.area: Walk-in / offline bookings break payout and reconciliation math
findings.issue: §6.1.6 lets staff create source=walkin, status=confirmed bookings with no online payment, but these still have an amount and still occupy inventory. If the payout/reconciliation job aggregates by venue, it must exclude walk-ins (no money flowed through VenuePro) — otherwise the platform either tries to disburse money it never collected or charges 1% on cash it never touched. The spec's payout description ('aggregates a venue's paid bookings') is ambiguous about whether the 1% applies to walk-ins, and there's no rule for it.
findings.recommendation: State explicitly that the 1% fee and payout apply ONLY to online-paid bookings settled through Xendit; walk-ins are inventory-only with zero platform money movement (or define a separate SaaS/no-fee treatment). Ensure reconciliation and PMK-37 withholding bases use online GMV only, and that venue dashboards clearly separate online revenue (net of fees) from offline cash.

crits.summary: A perfect Lighthouse-98 mobile score on the PUBLIC SSR Blade pages is achievable, but the spec treats "Livewire 3 PWA" as one uniform thing — and Lighthouse-98 is essentially incompatible with the interactive booking/availability widget where Livewire+Alpine hydration, morphdom diffing, and per-keystroke network round-trips dominate TBT on low-end Android. The two hardest, under-specified problems are (a) an offline strategy for inherently real-time availability (caching it risks selling already-booked slots) and (b) the self-hosting mandate colliding head-on with the spec's own "embedded Google Map" for discovery/local SEO. The data model, payments, and VPR are far more build-ready than the entire non-functional PWA/perf/SEO layer, which is currently aspirational bullet points rather than an engineering plan.

findings.area: Lighthouse scope: public SSR vs. interactive booking widget
findings.issue: The spec asserts a blanket 'Lighthouse >=98 (all categories, mobile) on key public pages' and CI-gates it, but conflates two very different page types. A static SSR Blade venue page can hit 98+ easily. The court-availability + slot-selection + payment page — the actual money page — is a Livewire component. Livewire 3 ships Alpine bundled inside a single ~100-130KB-min (~40KB gzip) livewire.js that must be parsed/executed and then HYDRATE the DOM (initial wire:snapshot JSON in the HTML + Alpine init). On a Lighthouse mobile run (emulated Moto-G-class CPU, 4x slowdown), JS parse/exec + hydration directly inflate Total Blocking Time and Time to Interactive, the two metrics most likely to push mobile Performance below 98. The interactive slot-picker is exactly where score risk concentrates, and the spec never separates these pages or sets a per-page budget. 'Key public pages' is undefined — if it excludes the booking widget, the gate is theater; if it includes it, the gate is likely unachievable as specified.
findings.recommendation: Split the budget explicitly: (1) Hard >=98 gate ONLY on the static SSR discovery/venue/court pages, which carry zero Livewire on first paint. (2) Make the slot-picker and payment pages SSR-Blade-first with progressive enhancement: render the full availability grid server-side as plain HTML+Alpine (no Livewire on these), and only mount Livewire for the actual hold/lock action, or use a single lazy Livewire island below the fold. (3) Set a numeric per-route budget in Lighthouse CI (e.g. TBT < 200ms, LCP < 2.5s, total JS < 60KB gzip on the booking route) and assert the booking route hits >=95, not 98, with a documented justification. (4) Defer Livewire's script (it already supports this) and never let Livewire JS be render-blocking. Treat the 98 number as a per-route contract, not a site-wide one.

findings.area: Offline strategy for DYNAMIC availability (stale-data / oversell risk)
findings.issue: Section 8.3 says 'cache GET availability/discovery responses (stale-while-revalidate)'. Availability is the single most time-sensitive datum in the system: a slot that was open 30 seconds ago may now be held/paid/confirmed. Serving cached availability from a service worker means a player can see and TAP an open slot that is already gone, or worse, an offline-cached page lets them believe they can book. SWR returns stale data first by definition. Combined with the Redis-locked hold flow (6.1), this creates a race where the SW-cached UI and server truth diverge, producing failed holds at best and user-perceived double-bookings at worst. The spec also doesn't say what 'offline booking' even means — you fundamentally cannot create a payment-backed hold offline, so an offline booking page is a dead end that will frustrate users.
findings.recommendation: Tier the cache by data volatility explicitly: (1) App shell + static assets + venue static info (name, address, photos, amenities) = cache-first/precache, safe. (2) Availability = NETWORK-ONLY (or network-first with a very short timeout) — never SWR; if offline, show an explicit 'availability needs a connection' state, not stale slots. (3) Always re-validate availability server-side at hold time inside the Redis lock+transaction (the spec already does this in 6.1 — make the SW NEVER bypass that). (4) Add a cache-busting/version header and a short max-age on the availability endpoint so even network-first doesn't serve a CDN/proxy-stale copy. (5) Define the offline UX as read-only browse + 'reconnect to book', not offline booking. Document that availability is intentionally excluded from offline.

findings.area: Self-hosting mandate vs. embedded Google Map (direct contradiction)
findings.issue: Two hard requirements collide. Section 8.1/4: 'zero external CDN/font requests', 'all libraries, assets, and fonts self-hosted (no CDN)'. Section 8.2 and 2: 'embedded map', 'Local SEO (NAP, embedded map)', and Google Maps named as the discovery channel. An embedded Google Map (iframe or JS API) is by definition an external request to maps.googleapis.com / gstatic — it loads remote JS, fonts, and raster/vector tiles, fires many third-party requests, and will both break the 'zero external request' invariant AND tank Lighthouse mobile (heavy third-party JS, render-blocking, large transfer). You cannot have both 'no external requests' and 'embedded Google Map'. Map tiles in general are the hardest asset to self-host.
findings.recommendation: Resolve the contradiction in the spec now. Options, pick one and write it down: (1) Static map IMAGE for the public/LCP path: render a self-hosted static map thumbnail (pre-generated raster, or a self-hosted MapLibre GL + self-hosted vector tiles / a self-hosted tile snapshot for the metro) and only load an interactive map on explicit user tap ('facade'/click-to-load pattern, like the well-known YouTube-facade technique). This keeps Lighthouse clean and zero external on first load. (2) For interactive maps, self-host MapLibre GL JS (open source, bundleable) + self-hosted tiles (OpenMapTiles/Protomaps PMTiles for Jakarta) — fully CDN-free and offline-capable, but adds real infra. (3) If business truly needs Google Maps branding, carve an explicit, documented exception to the no-CDN rule for the map ONLY, behind a click-to-load facade, and exclude that interaction from the Lighthouse-gated path. Do NOT leave 'embedded map' + 'no CDN' both as hard requirements.

findings.area: Service worker vs. Livewire wire:navigate SPA caching collision
findings.issue: The dashboard is a 'Livewire PWA' and Livewire's wire:navigate is the natural choice for SPA-feel navigation. But wire:navigate maintains its OWN in-memory page cache and does fetch()-based HTML swaps with morphdom, dispatching livewire:navigate/navigated lifecycle events instead of full page loads. A naively-configured service worker (e.g. Workbox navigation route) will intercept those fetches and can serve stale precached HTML, break the wire:snapshot/CSRF token freshness, double-cache, or desync Livewire's component state after a SW update. CSRF token rotation + cached HTML is a classic source of 419 errors. The spec lists 'service worker' and 'Livewire PWA' as if orthogonal; they actively interfere.
findings.recommendation: Decide the navigation model per surface: (1) Public marketplace: full SSR page loads or wire:navigate, with the SW handling ONLY static assets (cache-first by content hash) and a single offline fallback for navigations — do NOT precache HTML documents that contain CSRF tokens or wire:snapshots. (2) Authenticated dashboard: keep it network-first for documents; never serve cached authenticated HTML. (3) Explicitly scope the SW to skip Livewire's update/message endpoint and wire:navigate fetches (match on headers/X-Livewire). (4) Add a SW skipWaiting + clients.claim + version bump strategy and a 'new version available, reload' prompt so a deployed CSRF/asset change can't strand users on a stale shell. Test the 419-after-deploy scenario in CI.

findings.area: SSR SEO at marketplace scale: sitemap, canonicals, thin/duplicate pages, indexing budget
findings.issue: Section 8.2 lists schema.org types and 'Sitemap.xml + robots + canonical' but treats SEO as a checklist, not a scale problem. A two-sided marketplace generates a combinatorial URL space: /{sport}/{city}/{venue-slug}, /venue/{slug}, plus availability/date-filtered and search-result permutations. Risks: (a) thousands of near-empty venue/court pages at launch (Jakarta-first, few venues) = thin-content / low-quality signals; (b) faceted search (sport x location-radius x price x availability) creates infinite crawlable URL combinations that waste crawl budget and create duplicate content unless parameters are noindex/canonicalized; (c) availability changes constantly, so an Offer/AggregateOffer with price+availability in JSON-LD can be flagged as inconsistent if the rendered availability and the structured data drift; (d) no dynamic/paginated sitemap strategy is specified for a growing venue set. The single-DB venue_id row-level scope (4) also means the public side aggregates across all tenants — any tenant-scope leak in the public query is both a security and an SEO-cloaking risk.
findings.recommendation: (1) Decide crawl policy: canonical the clean /{sport}/{city}/{venue-slug} URL, and robots/noindex or canonicalize ALL faceted/filtered/date-param search URLs (rel=canonical to the unfiltered venue page; mark facet params in Search Console). (2) Generate a sitemap INDEX with per-section, paginated, lastmod-stamped sitemaps regenerated on venue/court change (cache it; it's a query, not a live render). (3) For Offer/AggregateOffer JSON-LD, only emit price ranges and 'by appointment'/InStock availability that won't churn per-minute; keep structured data and visible content consistent or you risk a structured-data penalty.
